perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information:” means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representatives including but not limited to: the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; any information that would be regarded as co nfidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (a) the business, affairs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans, intentions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; (b) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specifications, trade secrets or software of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of 7 (c) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information; but not including any information: (a) that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the Party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information); (b) was available to the Recipient on a non -confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) was, is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non -confidential basis from a person who, to the Recipient's knowledge, is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Party or otherwise prohibited from disclosing the information to the Recipient; (d) was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; (e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed; and/or (f) is developed by or for the Recipient indepe ndently of the information disclosed by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information:” means all information disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representatives related to the Purpose. This includes the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place. It does *not* include any information *specifically designated* as 'Confidential'.
perturbation.explanation: The revised definition of 'Confidential Information' now excludes information specifically labeled as 'Confidential.' This creates an in-text contradiction because Section 2, 'Obligations of Confidentiality', assumes all information fitting the *original* broad definition is protected. The change undermines the protection afforded to certain information, creating uncertainty about what information is actually subject to the NDA. The original definition protected all confidential information, the perturbation now protects everything BUT information designated as confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the P urpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly n ecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of the Recipient; (e) apply the same security meas ures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as the Recipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providing adequate protection from unauthori zed disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the other in tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by explicitly excluding information 'specifically designated' as confidential. This directly contradicts the contradicted text (Obligations of Confidentiality), which broadly protects the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information without such an exclusion. The original definition in 'Original Text' included virtually all confidential information, but the 'Changed Text' undermines this protection by creating an exception for designated confidential information. This introduces a clear inconsistency, making the original obligations unenforceable because what constitutes Confidential Information is now ambiguous, leading to uncertainty and a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclos ing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in relation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Informati on from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form ; and (c) certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflectin g, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed i ts obligations under this Agreement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient *may*, at its sole discretion: (a) destroy or return to the Disclos ing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in relation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Informati on from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form ; and (c) certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflectin g, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed i ts obligations under this Agreement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' in Section 3.1 makes the return or destruction of confidential information discretionary. This directly contradicts Section 2.1, which imposes a mandatory obligation on the Recipient to keep Confidential Information confidential. By making the return optional, the recipient could indefinitely retain confidential information, undermining the core purpose of the NDA and creating in-text contradiction. The original required returning/destroying the data, the perturbation now makes it optional.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the P urpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly n ecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of the Recipient; (e) apply the same security meas ures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as the Recipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providing adequate protection from unauthori zed disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the other in tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Recipient to destroy or return confidential information, but the changed text makes this action discretionary ('may'). This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which mandates that the Recipient 'shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential' and take specific actions to protect it. The 'may' in the Changed Text allows the Recipient to retain the information indefinitely, conflicting with the mandatory confidentiality obligations in the Contradicted Text. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists based on the explanation and the provided texts.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 2 years from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 2 years from the date of *disclosure* of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the start date of the 2-year obligation period from the 'date of this Agreement' to the 'date of disclosure' creates an in-text contradiction. This is because the agreement date is fixed, but confidential information may be disclosed at different times. Section 3.1 requires returning the information at termination and refers to 6.1, so the contradiction introduces ambiguity about how to calculate the lifespan of the NDA obligations. The original NDA had a fixed termination date, the perturbation now has a flexible one that relies on disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclos ing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in relation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Informati on from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form ; and (c) certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflectin g, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed i ts obligations under this Agreement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the trigger date for the obligation period from a fixed date ('date of this Agreement') to a variable date ('date of disclosure'). This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which outlines the requirements for returning or destroying confidential information at the termination of the agreement and references section 6.1. Since the termination date is now dependent on when disclosure happens, it creates ambiguity about the lifespan of NDA obligations and conflicts with how the return of information is handled. The contradicted text assumes a clear, fixed date for the obligations under 6.1, which is undermined by the changed text. This is an inconsistency, a type of in-text contradiction, as different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, as specified in the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 11.1 Any notice or other communication required to be given under this Agreement, shall be in writing and shall be delivered personally, or sent by pre -paid first class post or recorded delivery or by commercial courier, to each Party required to receive the notice or communication at its address as set out below: Kenway: Ian D. Kopp, 681 Riverside Drive, Augusta, Maine 04330 Company: _______________________________________________ or as otherwise specified by the relevant Party by notice in writing to each other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.1 Any notice or other communication required to be given under this Agreement, shall be delivered via email *only*. The email addresses are as follows: Kenway: [kenway@example.com] Company: [company@example.com] or as otherwise specified by the relevant Party by *oral* notice to each other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the required notice method to 'email only' and allowing changes to addresses via 'oral' notice creates an in-text contradiction with 11.3, which states 'A notice or other communication required to be given under this Agreement shall not be v alidly given if sent by e- mail.'. It makes the notice requirement confusing, since both sections are contradictory and one invalidates the other. The original only accepted written communications, the perturbation now accepts oral.
perturbation.contradicted_text: A notice or other communication required to be given under this Agreement shall not be v alidly given if sent by e- mail.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 11.1 to state that notices must be delivered via email only, and that address changes can be communicated orally. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text' which explicitly states that notices are not valid if sent via email. This creates a direct and irreconcilable conflict, thus representing a strong in-text contradiction. The explanation provided further emphasizes the conflict between the perturbed text and the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof. The Parties irrevocably agree that the Courts of Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of *Maine*. The Parties irrevocably agree that the Courts of *Maine* shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims). *However, any dispute regarding intellectual property will be handled in Delaware.*
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction to Maine, while specifying that IP disputes will be handled in Delaware, introduces an in-text contradiction. Section 4.1 states that intellectual property rights remain vested in the Disclosing Party, thus creating a source for potential legal action. The contradiction now creates ambiguity regarding where IP disputes should be resolved. The original used Delaware for all disputes, the perturbation now splits IP disputes with Maine.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each Party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Information are granted to the other Party and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this Agreemen t. Any background intellectual property rights subsisting in any information provided by either Party to the other shall remain vested in the Disclosing Party and the Disclosing Party shall grant to the Recipient a non -exclusive, worldwide, royalty free, r evocable license to use such background Intellectual Property Rights solely for the period of this Agreement and for the Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 5 of 7 Purpose only. Any foreground intellectual property rights generated by CP in respect of the Purpose shall vest in CP absolutely and the Company shall acquire no interest, right or title in respect of the same.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulated that all disputes would be handled in Delaware. The changed text alters this, specifying Maine as the governing law and jurisdiction but carves out an exception for intellectual property disputes, which are to be handled in Delaware. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that all intellectual property rights remain with the Disclosing Party. This creates ambiguity and a potential conflict as it is unclear which jurisdiction (Maine or Delaware) would handle disputes related to the enforcement or infringement of these intellectual property rights related to the disclosed information.
